title: The Disproportionate Assets Case: A Continuing Story! (8)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Repairs are confined to obvious OCR-created word splits and malformed joins. Source-printed dates, quantities, attributed testimony, and legal language are retained without harmonisation or inference. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: In the Jayalalithaa disproportionate-assets case under way in Bengaluru, government counsel Bhavani Singh continued to compile and present the details that had emerged through witness testimony.

Balakrishnan, IAS, Secretary of the Tamil Nadu Housing Board, testified with supporting evidence that when the first accused, Jayalalithaa, was Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu, Housing Board rules were violated to allot 1,800-square-foot housing plots to both V.N. Sudhakaran, the third accused, and Ilavarasi, the fourth accused. Although each had an annual income of several lakhs of rupees, false income certificates showing only Rs. 48,000 were obtained for them.

Government counsel Bhavani Singh also explained at length before the Special Court the testimony of an Indian Bank branch manager concerning bank accounts maintained in the names of various companies by Jayalalithaa, her close associate Sasikala, and Ilavarasi, and the irregular transfer of money among those companies.

Manickavasagam, manager of Indian Bank’s Abhiramapuram branch in Chennai, had testified: “Separate accounts were opened at our bank in the names of many companies, including Jaya Publications, Sasi Enterprises, Jaya Real Estate Company, Anjaneya Printers, Jaya Finance, Jaya Constructions, Ramaraj Agro Mills, Gopal Promoters, Jaya Farm House, Jaya Housing Development, Lakshmi Constructions, Namasivaya Constructions and Metal King Apartments, whose partners included the accused Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and Ilavarasi. Transactions took place in the accounts of all these companies from 1991 to 1996. When payments were made by cheque, the partners signed them. Money was also transferred from one company to another company’s bank account. Loans were obtained to buy land, machinery and buildings needed by the companies, and the partners signed the loan documents.”

Bhavani Singh explained that Manickavasagam had also provided detailed figures on the dates of the transfers, the dates on which bank loans were obtained, and whose residential address had been used as each company’s address.

Advocate R. Thiagarajan, appearing for Meadow Agro Farm, obtained statements from real-estate proprietors Kannan and Ganesan about the purchase of land for that company. When he later examined Kumar, a director of Riverway Agro Farm, Kumar said that land belonging to Jayakodi had been bought for Riverway Agro Farm through a real-estate agent named Siva on 10-9-1994.

He also stated that, through the same land agent, land belonging to the families of Veerabhadram, Muthu Nadar, Samuel Nadar, Ravi, Muthupandian, Mandhiram, Krishnan Konar and Padmanabha Reddiar had been purchased and registered on 21-12-1996; land belonging to Durai and the Kandasi Nadar family on 17-2-1992; and land belonging to the families of Raja Nadar, Shanmugam Nadar, Perinba Nadar and Shanmugasundaram Nadar on 18-8-1994.

Lex Properties Development Private Limited and other companies were attached in the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa and shown as properties of the accused. In March, these companies filed a special petition before the Bengaluru Special Court asking that they be released from the case and that the main trial be stayed until their petition was heard and decided.

After hearing the petition, Judge John Michael D’Cunha ruled: “I accept for hearing the request seeking the release of the companies attached in the disproportionate-assets case. But I dismiss the petition seeking a stay of the main trial.”

Condemning the request to stay the main case, he also imposed a fine of Rs. 10,000 “for wasting the court’s time” in his order of 14 March. Lex Properties appealed that Special Court order before the Karnataka High Court. The petition came before Justice Satyanarayana on 29-4-2014. After hearing both sides, he ruled:

“The Special Court at Bengaluru was constituted under the Supreme Court’s directions precisely so that the disproportionate-assets case could be concluded swiftly. The Supreme Court has also ordered that the trial be conducted daily. Even so, numerous petitions are being filed with the object of delaying the case. The petitioner’s request that Lex be released from the disproportionate-assets case is unacceptable, and I therefore dismiss the petition. The Special Court imposed a fine of Rs. 10,000 for wasting the court’s time. As that is too little, I increase it tenfold and impose a fine of Rs. 1 lakh. The amount must be paid within fifteen days.”

That was the judgment delivered by Karnataka High Court Justice Satyanarayana. What makes it still more remarkable is that the same Lex Properties had already once asked to be released from this case, and that request too had been rejected.

Lex Properties appealed to the Supreme Court. On 8-5-2014, Justices Chauhan, Chelameswar and Iqbal rejected the company’s request for a stay of the disproportionate-assets trial. The Supreme Court judges also censured the company for acting in a manner calculated to obstruct the trial.

Like Lex Properties, Meadow Agro Farm and Riverway Agro Farm filed separate petitions before the trial court asking that their applications for release from the disproportionate-assets case be heard and that the main trial be stayed in the meantime. Judge D’Cunha rejected the request for a stay and fined each company Rs. 5,000 for wasting the court’s time.
The judge then asked several questions concerning the petition filed by Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi seeking the lifting of the attachment on their properties.

He asked: “The disproportionate-assets case began in 1996. In 1998, you filed a petition before the Special Court constituted in Chennai to hear it. You sought the return of movable properties seized by the Tamil Nadu Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption during the case, entered as material evidence and placed under attachment. The court rejected your request in 2000. You appealed that order before the Madras High Court. On the second of this month, the court ruled on that petition. It set aside the Special Court’s attachment order and said that the Bengaluru Special Court must hear and decide the matter of the property attachment. Are you aware of this?”
Sasikala replied, “I do not know the full details of the proceedings on the petition we filed.”

The judge continued: “On 21 February, the government counsel filed a petition before this court asking that 1,116 kilograms of silver articles entered as material evidence be brought to Bengaluru. When you filed your reply to that petition, why did your side not inform this court that your petition before the Madras High Court seeking cancellation of the property attachment was still pending?” She replied, “I do not know.”
The judge turned to government counsel Bhavani Singh and asked, “Were you aware of this?” He too replied that he did not know.
The judge then put several questions to Sambandham, a Deputy Superintendent of Police in the Tamil Nadu Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption.

“Madras High Court Justice Jayapal expressed the same view in relation to this case in 2006, and Karnataka High Court Justice Jagannathan did so again in 2011. Although you knew this, why was a separate government counsel appointed when the petition filed by the accused against the attachment was heard in the Madras High Court? Why were the details of that petition withheld from the Karnataka government counsel appearing before this court?” the judge asked. Sasikala replied that all documents relating to the case had been given to the special counsel.

The testimony of Thiagarajan, Joint Commissioner in the Tamil Nadu Revenue Department, was read out. He had stated that the first accused, Jayalalithaa, owned 220 acres at Adilivakkam village near Uthukottai, where a separate processing centre had been established to process the fruit grown there for export. Sasikala, the second accused, owned forty acres in the same village, planted with mango trees. He testified that these lands were worth several lakhs of rupees.

In yet another attempt to prolong the Bengaluru disproportionate-assets case, Jayalalithaa’s counsel P. Kumar filed a petition and argued: “Priority must first be given to hearing the petitions seeking the release of the companies attached in the case. On the basis of the judgment delivered by Madras High Court Justice Aruna Jagadeesan on 2 April, the main trial should be suspended and the Bengaluru Special Court should first hear the matter concerning the companies. Only after it is established who owns the companies attached in the case can we prove that the allegations against our client are untrue.”

When a petition relating to this case came before Justice Jayapal in the Madras High Court in 2006, his order made it clear: “Wherever and in whichever court any petition connected with the disproportionate-assets case is heard, it must be brought to the notice of the special counsel appointed by the Karnataka government on the Karnataka High Court’s recommendation. Moreover, he alone has the right to appear and argue it.”

When another petition in the same case came before Justice Arumugasamy in the Madras High Court in 2013, he ruled that the court could not interfere with the decision of the Bengaluru Special Court hearing the disproportionate-assets case.

Arguing for government counsel Bhavani Singh, advocate Murugesh Maradi said: “In light of the judgments delivered by Justice Jayapal in 2006 and Justice Arumugasamy in 2013, the judgment delivered by Justice Aruna Jagadeesan cannot in any way bind the Bengaluru Special Court. The petition filed by the accused should therefore be rejected.”

In his order of 7-5-2014 on the petition seeking the release of the companies from the disproportionate-assets case, Judge John Michael D’Cunha clarified: “That petition has already been admitted for hearing. The request to stay the main trial until that hearing is completed cannot be accepted. Both matters will proceed simultaneously. A judgment on the petition concerning the companies will be delivered before judgment is given in the main case.”
Summing up 2,500 pages of evidentiary documents before the Special Court, government counsel Bhavani Singh said:

“I am submitting a 2,500-page compilation of witness statements against the accused and evidence of the assets they accumulated. Before the period covered by the case, the accused had only seventeen properties. During that period, the number rose to 306. There is documentary evidence that 289 of them were acquired through various means beyond their known income.
“In particular, the increase in the economic position of the accused during the period bears no relation to their position before it.
“Every statement given by the witnesses in the disproportionate-assets case is true. The court must accept them!” Bhavani Singh insisted.
It is worth remembering that this very Bhavani Singh attempted in many ways to delay the trial.

This is not the only case in which Jayalalithaa, caught in a corruption case, obtained adjournment after adjournment for years and escaped a decision. The same is true of several other cases. They too have continued year after year.

With all these events in mind, at an election campaign meeting on 17-4-2014 I raised this vital question: “Have matters reached a point where one can say, ‘If we come, you must conduct the case; if we do not, you must adjourn it; otherwise, you must withdraw it’? Is this Jayalalithaa’s realm, or the realm of justice?”

All their courage comes from this thought: “However many crores in assets we may accumulate, we can obtain adjournments in court, drag the case out without limit, and spend as many years as we please. Whatever we do, the people will trust us and vote for us.” That is the sixteen-year history of the disproportionate-assets case!

All Tamil Nadu awaits the verdict in this case. Lawyers and those concerned with the judiciary throughout India are watching it closely. The day of judgment is not far away! The veil of mist that obscures our sight must lift! The world will know the truth!
(Concluded)
With affection,
M.K.
